title: Grounding techniques explained
citation: Najavits, L.M. (2002). Seeking safety: A treatment manual for PTSD and substance abuse. Guilford Press.
content:
Grounding techniques are strategies that help a person anchor their attention to the present moment and their immediate physical environment. Their primary purpose is to interrupt or reduce experiences of dissociation, emotional overwhelm, flashback states, or intense anxiety, all of which involve the mind becoming absorbed in internal experiences, past events, or feared future scenarios rather than remaining connected to the here and now.

The rationale for grounding is that directing attention to concrete, verifiable sensory or physical information in the present environment can reduce the intensity of distressing internal states. When the nervous system is highly activated, returning attention to what can be seen, heard, touched, and smelled in the room provides an anchor that competes with the escalating internal spiral.

Grounding techniques fall into three broad types. Sensory grounding involves engaging the physical senses to orient to the present moment. The widely used 5-4-3-2-1 technique asks a person to identify five things they can see, four they can touch, three they can hear, two they can smell, and one they can taste, moving progressively through the senses to build a detailed sensory picture of the immediate environment. Physical grounding uses the body itself as an anchor: pressing feet firmly into the floor, holding a cold object, or feeling the texture of a surface. Cognitive grounding involves mental tasks that require focused attention, such as reciting a familiar poem, counting backwards, or describing an object in detail.

Grounding techniques are particularly useful for people experiencing anxiety, post-traumatic stress disorder, or dissociative symptoms. They were developed and systematised within trauma-focused treatment frameworks, including Lisa Najavits's Seeking Safety programme, which was designed for people with co-occurring PTSD and substance use difficulties. Grounding is taught as a skill to be practised when calm so that it becomes more accessible when distress is high.
